title: Add support for building macOS pkg installer
body:
it installs podman and supporting binaries along with
qemu to have a functioning podman install using a pkg

podman and podman-mac-helper  is compiled from source

gvproxy binary is downloaded from its github releases
and qemu from github release of containers-contribs/qemu-macos-build

[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

Signed-off-by: Anjan Nath <kaludios@gmail.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
NONE
```

Needs #14324

comments.body: @anjannath Looks like the validate stage does not like the format of your bash scripts.

> @anjannath Looks like the validate stage does not like the format of your bash scripts.

I think it was a missing new line after the shebang, re-pushed lets see
@gbraad Thoughts?

This is the PR we propose from crc.
I already approved it from my end, but have no rights here.

LGTM
@baude @mheon PTAL

Hi @rhatdan CI is green now, thanks.

I also wanted to get your views about the `qemu` build it uses from (http://github.com/containers-contribs/qemu-macos-build).

We'd like to move that repo under the containers org and have it hooked to the CI so that we can have automated builds of `qemu` and releases that we can use with the pkg, since that will also need to be maintained.

Or is it preferred to not install `qemu` as part of this installer and leave it to the user to setup, but probably that means the users who can't do `brew install` on their machines for some reason, will not have a full podman setup ootb after using this installer.

Qemu is either part of the installer, or there is a a separate qemu
installer? For us CRC we are not planning to use qemu as vfkit and virtiofs
look very promising at this point.

Perhaps it is possible for the moment to include a qemu binary and over
time move to vfkit?

@anjannath I am fine with move that into containers.

> @anjannath I am fine with move that into containers. 

Lets make that a dependency of this pr. Who can move/accept this? We will do so tomorrow:
`containers/podman-machine-qemu` ?

----

Done
@gbraad Also updated the PR to use the qemu build from https://github.com/containers/podman-machine-qemu/releases/tag/6.2.0-1
@vrothberg @mheon @giuseppe PTAL
I can't test without a Mac.
@ashley-cui PTAL

I was able to build the installer on my m1, but am unable to run it without the "install rosetta prompt" which shouldn't be the case. Looks like the install script is using aarch64 and arm arches, so this is strange that I'm getting this error. Anyone have any ideas?
I was able to build and run installer on may intel mac.

> I was able to build the installer on my m1, but am unable to run it without the "install rosetta prompt" which shouldn't be the case. Looks like the install script is using aarch64 and arm arches, so this is strange that I'm getting this error. Anyone have any ideas?

looks like the `gvproxy` binary we are using from https://github.com/containers/gvisor-tap-vsock/releases/download/v0.3.0/gvproxy-darwin is `x86` we need to publish arm64 binaries of `gvproxy`
Podman has no way to update containers.conf, but we can read multiple.  Not sure if it supports dropin files yet.
Podman will read dropin configs in /etc/containers/containers.conf/config.d and $HOME/.config/containers/containers.conf.d/

```
man containers.conf
containers.conf(5)                                                                                            configuration                                                                                           containers.conf(5)

NAME
       containers.conf - The container engine configuration file specifies default configuration options and command-line flags for container engines.

DESCRIPTION
       Container engines like Podman & Buildah read containers.conf file, if it exists and modify the defaults for running containers on the host. containers.conf uses a TOML format that can be easily modified and versioned.

       Container  engines read the /usr/share/containers/containers.conf and /etc/containers/containers.conf, and /etc/containers/containers.conf.d/.conf files if they exist. When running in rootless mode, they also read $HOME/.con‐
       fig/containers/containers.conf and $HOME/.config/containers/containers.conf.d/.conf files.

       Fields specified in containers conf override the default options, as well as options in previously read containers.conf files.

       Config files in the .d directories, are added in alpha numeric sorted order and must end in .conf.

       Not all options are supported in all container engines.
```
```

This means podman desktop could drop a file into those directories and own it.
Any fields specified in this file would override the system defaults.

> are added in alpha numeric order

This means this defines the priority for overriding the values of `containers.conf`?

i think the installer should create `/etc/containers/containers.conf.d/99-podman-installer.conf`. It can be safely overwritten during upgrades, users can override using `~/.config/containers/containers.conf` if needed
You mean  ~/.config/containers/containers.conf
Updated the `postinstall` script to create the drop-in config at `/etc/containers/containers.conf.d/99-podman-installer.conf`

Hello, I'm wondering why it installs the podman CLI into /Applications folder

usually /Applications is for Desktop app, not CLI

Also /Applications are displayed in Launchpad

Such CLI tools usually go to `/opt` or `/usr/local` folder. (like all brew stuff and macPorts)

> Hello, I'm wondering why it installs the podman CLI into /Applications folder
> 
> usually /Applications is for Desktop app, not CLI
> 
> Also /Applications are displayed in Launchpad
> 
> Such CLI tools usually go to `/opt` or `/usr/local` folder. (like all brew stuff and macPorts)

No particular reason, we just needed the full path to the target location and `/Applications` was a nice short path and also guaranteed to exist on macOS that we used during the testing and did not change it again.

This can be easily changed by modifying the `--install-location` flag for the `pkgbuild` command ([code](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/14540/files#diff-4d495b0eed263a462b261e7c4aa56d0252665d48b69131b94db1e61a718414b6R44)) we just need to decide on which location to use.

I'll update the PR to install in `/opt/podman` instead. (would also love to hear suggestions from others around this)

I'm also wondering what is happening when user has installed podman with brew.

Do we prevent installation by default on such case or user will have two podman installed on his computer ?

> I'm also wondering what is happening when user has installed podman with brew.
> 
> Do we prevent installation by default on such case or user will have two podman installed on his computer ?

atm, user will have two podman installed on his computer, i think we can do some checking to see if `podman` is on path and abort installation in that case with a message but this is not done currently

> i think we can do some checking to see if podman is on path and abort installation in that case with a message but this is not done currently

I think you should be allowed to install both, but it is up to the tooling to determine and use the correct (or specific) version/location.

> I'll update the PR to install in /opt/podman instead. (would also love to hear suggestions from others around this)

let me know when it's done, I'll test on my computer.

> > I'll update the PR to install in /opt/podman instead. (would also love to hear suggestions from others around this)
> 
> let me know when it's done, I'll test on my computer.

hi @benoitf its updated now, give it a try, thanks!
I've noticed that this installer is using QEMU 6.0.2 while brew installation is using QEMU 7

I've tried the installer

1. podman is not available in my PATH after the install (even if I open a new shell)
2. podman machine init command is not working

```
/opt/podman/bin/podman machine init                                                                                                                                                                
Extracting compressed file
Error: fork/exec /opt/podman/qemu/bin/qemu-img: bad CPU type in executable
```

I had to specify ARCH 
```
make ARCH=amd64 NO_CODESIGN=1 pkginstaller
```
to make it downloading the correct files

It seems it's not using qemu from /opt as the process is using `/usr/local/bin/qemu-system-x86_64`

Also I noticed that `podman-machine-qemu-amd64-6.2.0-1.tar.xz` does not contain  QEMU binaries

```
$ tar ztvf tmp-download/podman-machine-qemu-aarch64-6.2.0-1.tar.xz| grep qemu-system                                                                                                                 -rwxr-xr-x  0 anjan  admin 16853104 Jun 13 09:09 ./bin/qemu-system-aarch64
-rwxr-xr-x  0 anjan  admin 13019064 Jun 13 09:09 ./bin/qemu-system-x86_64
```
while the amd64 file:
```
$ tar ztvf tmp-download/podman-machine-qemu-amd64-6.2.0-1.tar.xz| grep qemu-system
```

nothing 

Size is also very different:
```
35M Jun 22 14:25 podman-machine-qemu-aarch64-6.2.0-1.tar.xz
17M Jun 22 14:19 podman-machine-qemu-amd64-6.2.0-1.tar.xz
```

@benoitf Thanks for testing out, will try to reproduce the issues and update the PR accordingly

> It seems it's not using qemu from /opt as the process is using /usr/local/bin/qemu-system-x86_64

i think this is how `podman` works, if the `qemu-system-*` binary is found on path it uses that, and if not then only goes ahead to look at the list of `helper_binaries_dir`, since you already had qemu installed from brew and added to path it picked that.

> 1. podman is not available in my PATH after the install (even if I open a new shell)

Which shell are you using? currently the installer tries to prepend the PATH variable for common shells, https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/14540/files#diff-2c01a0a2da8e4898ed43aa57ba70451351b8eacfdbee1e2badae4a883a025a80R5-R21

@anjannath AFAIK the main issue is that all `qemu-system-*`are not in the `podman-machine-qemu-amd64-6.2.0-1.tar.xz` archive while they're in the arm64 archive

I'm using zsh / oh-my-zsh
```
$  env | grep SHELL                                                                                                                                                                                 SHELL=/bin/zsh
```
The amd64 package does not include the `qemu-system-x86_64`, but in the M1 release there is an unnecessary `qemu-system-x86_64` target ;-)

@anjannath What else is needed here?
I think we are just waiting for approval, right?

@gbraad there was issues with the `qemu` build, and i wanted to update it to 7.0.0, made a new [release](https://github.com/containers/podman-machine-qemu/releases/tag/v7.0.0-2) for it and updated this PR to use it.

while testing i found the following behaviour on the first start after fresh install (happens with both the qemu builds 7.0.0 and the older ones):

`podman machine start` errors out as shown below, with `--log-level debug` the qemu monitor is also launched and there i can see the vm is still starting, although after a `podman machine stop` if i try `podman machine start` again it works as expected.

Not sure what causes this, maybe `gvproxy` is not started at the right time?

$ podman machine start --log-level debug

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *anjannath*, *benoitf*, *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

FYI I've noticed that if I remove /opt/podman and reinstall podman using brew

then I can't use podman

```
Starting machine "podman-machine-default"
Error: unable to start host networking: "could not find \"gvproxy\" in one of [/opt/podman/bin /opt/podman/qemu/bin].  To resolve this error, set the helper_binaries_dir key in the `[engine]` section of containers.conf to the directory containing your helper binaries."
```

this is due to the file `/etc/containers/containers.conf.d/99-podman-machine.conf` that is added by this installer with the content

```
[engine]
    helper_binaries_dir = [
        "/opt/podman/bin",
        "/opt/podman/qemu/bin"
    ]
```

so probably uninstall section should document that but it might be interesting as well to not have the file there if possible

@benoitf I could reproduce this, but unfortunately i can't think of a way to remove the config file during uninstall :(

but when `brew` installs the helper binaries it also adds them to PATH so i am not sure why podman isn't using them from there.

looking at the code here:
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0de7e4662e6d574fe382714cb6664a4519771f88/pkg/machine/qemu/machine.go#L1213

it's explicitly set to not look at the PATH to find the helper binaries, i think if we pass a `true`  this'll work?

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0de7e4662e6d574fe382714cb6664a4519771f88/vendor/github.com/containers/common/pkg/config/config.go#L1228
In short, brew conflicts here?
@gbraad yes this installer breaks brew setup

It is usually the other way around...
They will ALWAYS share the config files, as they are on the same system.

So in that case, fail when brew Podman is detected ?

@gbraad well the problem is that this installer creates a file in something that is not in /opt/podman so even following uninstalling steps by removing `/opt/podman` there is still a file in `/etc/containers/containers.conf.d/` that is blocking brew

I understand, these are the helper binaries. But if the order was different they would still not play nicely. That is kind of a user issue. It could be detected and then fail the install, but if something is done by the brew install it could damage a general system wide config.

The ideal would be to use an install specific configuration? Is that possible... Sounda like a lof of workaround.

there is no way to say that when we run podman from /opt/podman it reads also /opt/podman/etc folder to search for a containers.conf file with these helper config ? instead to put file in `/etc/containers/containers.conf.d/`
We do not recompile podman(-remote) with a prefix. This is the regular version. So that would be a question for @rhatdan or @baude.
Would this help out? https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/13372 Instead of setting helper binaries dir in a custom containers.conf, you could set it at compile-time
I think @ashley-cui suggestion would be really nice

> Found a solution to the Rosetta problem! However, even after that, podman isn't installed into my path and going to /opt/podman 

yes it installs in /opt/podman and adds `/opt/podman/bin to PATH`, by appending `PATH=/opt/podman/bin:$PATH` to the shell config file in case of zsh ~/.zshrc and/or ~/.zshenv

> and running ./podman results in `zsh: permission denied: ./podman`

is it missing executable permissions? maybe we need to explicitly make it executable in the `postinstall` script.
